locked_facts: Sign convention: distances measured from pole, along principal axis
For flat surface (R → ∞): n₁/u + n₂/v = 0, giving apparent depth formula
This formula leads to the lens maker's equation when applied to both surfaces of a lens
Real image formed when refracted rays actually converge; virtual when they appear to diverge
Applicable to convex and concave refracting surfaces
truth_statements: The refraction formula n₁/u + n₂/v = (n₂-n₁)/R relates object and image distances for a single refracting surface
Applying this formula to both surfaces of a thin lens and combining gives the lens maker's equation
For a flat refracting surface R is infinite and the formula reduces to the apparent depth relation
The formula uses the same sign convention as for mirrors with all distances measured from the pole
Both real and virtual images can be formed depending on the object position and the curvature

aha_moment.what_to_show: Light crossing a single curved glass-air interface. Applying n₁/u + n₂/v = (n₂-n₁)/R to both surfaces of a lens one after another — the image of surface 1 becomes the object for surface 2. Combining gives the lens maker's equation 1/f = (n-1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂).
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two successive applications of the single-surface formula collapsing into the lens maker's equation
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students memorise the lens formula without understanding its origin — seeing it derived from two single-surface refractions makes it feel logical, not arbitrary

simulation_states.label: Single refracting surface
simulation_states.description: Light from object in medium 1 (n₁) hits a curved surface separating medium 2 (n₂). Refraction bends rays. Image formed in medium 2.
simulation_states.emphasis: n₁/u + n₂/v = (n₂-n₁)/R governs the image location

simulation_states.label: Flat surface case
simulation_states.description: R → ∞ (flat surface). Formula gives n₁/u = -n₂/v → apparent depth = real depth × (n₁/n₂). A coin in water appears closer to the surface.
simulation_states.emphasis: Flat surface is a special case — gives the apparent depth formula

simulation_states.label: Building the lens formula
simulation_states.description: Apply single-surface formula to surface 1 of a lens (air→glass), then surface 2 (glass→air). Image of surface 1 = object for surface 2. Combine: 1/f = (n-1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂).
simulation_states.emphasis: The lens maker's equation comes from TWO applications of the single-surface formula

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts n₁, n₂, R, and object position. Image position and nature (real/virtual) shown. Toggle between convex and concave surfaces. Build a lens from two surfaces.
simulation_states.emphasis: Change curvature and refractive indices — see how image position changes

common_misconceptions.belief: The lens formula and single-surface formula are independent formulas
common_misconceptions.correction: The lens maker's equation is derived by applying n₁/u + n₂/v = (n₂-n₁)/R to both surfaces of a lens sequentially. They are the same physics — the lens formula is a consequence of single-surface refraction.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show step-by-step derivation from two single-surface applications to lens formula

common_misconceptions.belief: Refraction at a curved surface always forms a real image
common_misconceptions.correction: Both real and virtual images can form depending on object position, curvature, and refractive indices. When refracted rays diverge, the image is virtual.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show cases producing real and virtual images at the same curved surface
